<!-- question-type: prepare -->
### Exercise 2: A First Peek at Some Data

David Jones now wants to use **last year's data** to improve inventory decisions for the upcoming holiday season.

The data you have to work from are provided below:


| Week Commencing | Region    | Starting Inventory | Units Delivered | Units Sold | Ending Inventory | Price (AUD) | Promotion (Y/N) |
|-----------------|-----------|------------------|----------------|------------|-----------------|-------------|----------------|
| 25 Nov          | Sydney    | 500              | 100            | 100        | 500             | 50          | N              |
| 25 Nov          | Melbourne | 500              | 100            | 150        | 450             | 50          | N              |
| 2 Dec           | Sydney    | 500              | 100            | 180        | 420             | 45          | Y              |
| 2 Dec           | Melbourne | 450              | 100            | 220        | 330             | 45          | Y              |
| 9 Dec           | Sydney    | 420              | 100            | 150        | 370             | 45          | Y              |
| 9 Dec           | Melbourne | 330              | 100            | 100        | 330             | 45          | Y              |
| 16 Dec          | Sydney    | 370              | 100            | 400        | 70              | 40          | Y (Christmas)  |
| 16 Dec          | Melbourne | 330              | 100            | 400        | 30              | 40          | Y (Christmas)  |
| 23 Dec          | Sydney    | 70               | 100            | 120        | 50              | 50          | N              |
| 23 Dec          | Melbourne | 30               | 100            | 150        | -20 (backorder) | 50          | N              |


Before analysts run any code, they ask a simple question:

> **Can we trust this dataset enough to make a decision?**

Look at the David Jones data above and answer the questions below.

**(a)** Identify **5 potential issues** in the table. For each issue, classify it as:

* **1. Will break analysis/code**: formatting and/or type problems 
* **2. Unclear meaning**: we need a formal definition from the business
* **3. Decision risk**: could bias conclusions or lead to a bad recommendation

Fill in this template:

| Issue you noticed | Category (1/2/3) | Why it matters (1 sentence) |
| ----------------- | ---------------- | --------------------------- |
|                   |                  |                             |
|                   |                  |                             |
|                   |                  |                             |
|                   |                  |                             |
|                   |                  |                             |
|                   |                  |                             |

**(b)** Write one question you would email to the data owner today that would most improve your confidence in using the data to make a recommendation.



<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->
::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.teaching-block}

::: {.teaching-block-header}
Teaching Note
:::

::: {.teaching-block-body}

🎯 **Learning Objective** 
Students should:

Help students internalise a core analyst habit: before you compute anything, check whether the data is trustworthy and well-defined enough to support a decision. Students should leave with (i) a concrete sense of “data issues,” (ii) vocabulary for rows/columns/types, and (iii) practice distinguishing code-breaking vs meaning vs decision-risk problems.
✅   **Core Concepts to Highlight**

List them here


💬 **Suggested In-Class Prompts** (if needed)


NA - prep exercise

📌 **Common Misunderstandings**

Students list vague issues (“data is messy”) without pointing to a specific column/value.

- Nudge: “Show me the cell/column that creates the problem.”

They treat all issues as ‘code’ issues.

- Reframe: “Some issues won’t crash R—but can still crash your recommendation.”

They assume negative inventory is impossible.

- Clarify: negative can be a legitimate encoding of backorders, but it must be defined and stored numerically.


:::

:::

:::
<!-- END PROFILE:r-teaching-guide -->

<!-- BEGIN PROFILE:r-solutions -->
::: {.content-visible when-profile="r-solutions" when-profile="r-teaching-guide"}

::: {.solution-block}

::: {.solution-block-header}
Solution
:::

::: {.solution-block-body}

**(a)**

| Issue you noticed | Category (1/2/3) | Why it matters (1 sentence) |
|---|---:|---|
| `Week Commencing` has no year (e.g., “25 Nov”) | 2 | Without a year it’s ambiguous and hard to join to other data or compare across seasons. |
| `Week Commencing` is text, not a real date | 1 | R will treat it as character unless we parse it, which breaks sorting/plotting over time. |
| Column name `Price (AUD)` contains spaces + parentheses | 1 | Students will make coding mistakes; it’s harder to reference consistently. |
| `Promotion (Y/N)` mixes values like `Y` and `Y (Christmas)` | 1 | Mixed formats make the column messy to analyse and easy to misclassify. |
| `Ending Inventory` includes `-20 (backorder)` (number + text) | 1 | The column won’t import as numeric, so basic calculations (min, comparisons) will fail. |
| Meaning of negative ending inventory is unclear | 2 | Does `-20` mean unmet demand, orders placed, or an accounting adjustment? Interpretation changes recommendations. |
| Timing is unclear: do deliveries arrive before sales or after sales? | 2 | Inventory dynamics change completely depending on timing, affecting “stockout” interpretation. |
| `Units Sold` may be constrained by inventory (stockouts) | 3 | Observed sales may understate true demand, so forecasting from sales alone can mislead. |
| Price changes coincide with promotion changes | 3 | Does the price include the promotion?. |

**(b)**
Pick one of these:

Timing (highest value):
“Do deliveries occur before sales for that week (available to sell immediately), or after sales (arrive at end of week)?”

Backorder definition:
“When Ending Inventory is negative (e.g., -20), does that represent unmet demand/backorders, and if so how is it recorded and fulfilled?”

Promotion definition:
“What exactly counts as a Promotion = Y here—does it mean a discount, an in-store campaign, online promotion, or something else—and is Y (Christmas) a different type or intensity?”
:::

:::

:::
<!-- END PROFILE:r-solutions -->
